title: Crystalline metal flakes: Platforms for advanced plasmonics and hybrid 2D material architectures
authors: Sergejs Boroviks, Siarhei Zavatski, Thorsten Feichtner, Jer-Shing Huang, Olivier J. F. Martin, Bert Hecht, N. Asger Mortensen
abstract: Crystalline noble metal flakes are emerging as versatile platforms in nanophotonics, enabling a broad range of optical phenomena and applications. Their atomically flat surfaces, high crystallinity, and superior optical quality open new avenues in advanced plasmonics, quantum light generation, and hybrid photonic systems. In contrast to conventional polycrystalline metal films, which typically suffer from higher optical losses due to grain boundaries, surface roughness, and structural disorder, these monocrystalline flakes provide minimal scattering and enhanced performance. They serve as templates for precise nanostructuring through techniques like focused-ion beam (FIB) milling and are crucial for advanced applications in sensing and optoelectronics. Additionally, they facilitate frontier research in quantum plasmonics, enabling fundamental studies of nonlocal optical effects and the generation of nonclassical light. Furthermore, the well-defined $\{111\}$ facets of these flakes host Tamm--Shockley surface states that support 2D plasmons coexisting with bulk modes. At near-infrared wavelengths and beyond, crystalline flakes act as nearly ideal metallic mirrors, featuring surface roughness limited only to atomic terrace steps, making them highly suitable for integration with 2D materials in hybrid photonic architectures. This review surveys the key roles these flakes play, highlighting recent developments and discussing future prospects while emphasizing their unique benefits in addressing fundamental and applied challenges in modern nanophotonics.
summary: This review examines the emergence of crystalline metal flakes as a superior alternative to traditional polycrystalline films for nanophotonics. It highlights how their high crystallinity and atomic flatness reduce optical losses and enable precise nanostructuring. The paper covers various chemical synthesis routes, fabrication methods like FIB and EBL, and the integration of these flakes with 2D materials. Ultimately, it positions these flakes as essential platforms for advanced applications in sensing, optoelectronics, and quantum plasmonics.
main_problem: Addressing the limitations of conventional polycrystalline metal films, such as high Ohmic losses and surface roughness, by utilizing crystalline metal flakes for advanced plasmonics and hybrid 2D material architectures.
main_result: Crystalline metal flakes provide superior structural definition and reduced scattering, enabling enhanced plasmonic performance, precise nanostructuring via FIB/EBL, and effective integration with 2D materials.
method: The paper reviews chemical synthesis methods (polyol, Brust-Schiffrin, etc.), nanofabrication techniques (FIB, EBL, etching), and characterization tools (SEM, TEM, SNOM, ellipsometry).
model_or_system: The primary system consists of quasi-monocrystalline noble metal flakes (Au, Ag, Al, Cu) characterized by atomically flat surfaces, well-defined {111} facets, and stacking faults.
key_observables: Surface Plasmon Polariton (SPP) propagation length, complex permittivity, surface roughness, aspect ratio, and nonlinear optical responses.
important_parameters: Thickness (from ~1.9 nm to >1 um), lateral size (up to ~250 um), and crystal facets ({111} vs {100}).
main_assumptions: The flakes are treated as quasi-monocrystals due to the presence of twin planes/stacking faults required for anisotropic growth.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Morphology and crystal structure of Au flakes; Fig 2: Linear optical properties and SPP propagation; Fig 3: Comparison of Ga-FIB vs He-FIB milling quality; Fig 4: EBL fabrication flow and patterned nanostructures; Fig 5: Examples of micromanipulation techniques.
paper_structure: The review begins by contrasting polycrystalline vs. crystalline films, moves to the structural and chemical synthesis of flakes, discusses fabrication and nanostructuring techniques, explores integration with 2D materials, and concludes with future prospects.
why_interesting: The paper is highly relevant to quantum optics and nanophotonics due to its discussion on quantum plasmonics, nonclassical light generation, and the manipulation of light-matter interactions in hybrid architectures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Morphology and crystal structure of Au flakes. (a) Bright-field, (b) dark-field optical micrographs and (c) 75-degree tilted view SEM of a typical Au flake sample; the inset in (c) shows a close-up of the indicated corner of the flake. (d) A schematic drawing of an Au flake morphology, exhibiting a typical distribution of the two lattice planes, {100} and {111}-type, as side facets. Additionally, stacking fault defects in the middle are illustrated. Growth model: I-III Chemical reactions leading to the reduction of Au atoms that can form crystal seeds (IVa) or bind to the surface of an already existing crystal (IVb). (e) TEM image of a small flake sample. (f) High-resolution TEM...

Fig. 2. Linear optical properties of crystalline flakes measured using far-field techniques (a–c) and using SNOM (d,e). (a) Micro-ellipsometry measurements of the complex permittivity of a thin Au flake in comparison with measurements on a bulk crystal from Olmon et al. [131]. (b) Excitation and scattering of a SPP from the edges of an Ag flake. (c) illustration of the SPP propagation length measurement on a surface of an Ag flake with milled in- and out-coupling slits using (left top) white light interference and (left bottom) direct laser excitation and scattering; (right) comparison of these measurements against other experimental reports. SNOM measurements of SPP propagating on an Au...

Fig. 3. Comparison of Ga and He FIB milling-based fabrication in poly- and monocrys- talline Au nanostructures. (a) (top) Plasmonic waveguides and (bottom) bull’s eye resonator and (b) V-grooves fabricated with Ga ion FIB. (c) He ion milling of fine fea- tures (asymmetric gap antenna) in a blank predefined with Ga FIB. (d) He ion-polished V-groove plasmonic waveguides premilled with Ga FIB. (a) Reprinted with permission from Ref. [11], (b) from Ref. [28], (c) from Ref. [150], and (d) from Ref. [149].

Fig. 4. Examples of EBL-based fabrication of crystalline nanostructures. (a) Typical fabrication process flow for EBL-based nanofabrication with crystalline Au flakes. (b) AFM image of an Au flake patterned with a concentric ring shape using method shown in (a) and cysteamine as an etchant; lower part of the panel shows a profile along the indicated dashed. (c) SEM and (d) dark-field optical microscope images of crystalline Au nanoantenna arrays fabricated using EBL and RIE. (e) SEM image of a crystalline Au nanodisk array fabricated using EBL and IBE. (a,b) Reprinted with permission from Ref. [76], (c,d) from Ref. [153], and (e,f) from Ref. [174].

Fig. 5. Examples of crystalline flake micromanipulation. (a) Schematic diagram of Au flake transfer from a glass onto another substrate using PDMS stamp. (b) Planar gap plasmon waveguide comprised of Au flake transferred atop of another flake with a dielectric spacer; inset shows a close-up SEM image of the coupling structure FIB-milled in the lower flake. (c) Integration of Au flake with optical fiber for a background-free excitation of plasmonic nanocavities by the evanescent fields: inset shows an Au flake transferred on the core of an angled fibered tip. Fig. 6. Selected application examples of monocrystalline flakes in nano- and micro- devices. (a) Micrograph of stretchable and bendable electrodes deposited on "Ecoflex" substrates; inset shows close-up SEM image of the indicated area. (b) Au flakes as a force sensor: (right) plot shows variation of in-plane electric resistance of the film upon finger touch or air nozzle blow, insets illustrate applied stimuli, and (right) schematically depict deformation of the sample upon applied mechanical force. Fig. 7. Selected application examples of monocrystalline flakes in nano- and micro- devices. (a) Plasmonic two-wire transmission lines for mode conversion: SEM image (left), scattering diagrams (top) and far-field scattering images (bottom) for orthogonal polarization excitations. (b) SEM image (top) and SNOM scan (bottom) of broadband plasmonic logic gates based on curved gratings. (c) Schematic illustration of an on-chip all-optical information processing device: directional signal transmittance mediated by 2D plasmonic eigenmodes supported by crystalline cavities. Fig. 8. Selected application examples of monocrystalline flakes in bio and chemical sensing, and catalysis. (a) Ultraspecific attomolar detection of C-reactive protein biomarkers. Flowchart shows immobilization process and graph on the right hand- side show SERS spectra of rhodamine B isothiocyanate, which is bound to the Au nanoparticles, and serves as a marker (SERS nanotag). Fig. 10. Anisotropic SHG from crystalline Au surface. (a,b) Polar plot of second- harmonic intensity as a function of crystal orientation angle for co-polarized (black) and cross-polarized (red) signals at polycrystalline surface (a) and {111}-type surface (b). Insets in (a,b) show schematic diagrams of the first few layers of atom arrangements at the two types of surfaces. (c,d) Spectrum of the nonlinear reflection from polycrystalline surface (c) and {111}-type surface (d). The inset in (d) shows a zoom-in of the measured NPL spectral region (450–550 nm). Curve colors indicate excitation wavelength: 780 nm (blue), 800 nm (green), 820 nm (yellow), and 840 nm (red). Reprinted with...

Fig. 11. Nonlinear photoluminescence from Au flakes. (a) Illustration of an Au {111}-type illuminated by a femtosecond Gaussian pulse, along with the resulting nonlinear photoluminescence. (b) Dynamics of the occupied density of electronic states upon optical excitation. From the equilibrium state at room temperature, electrons are promoted to higher energy levels, and the associated holes are subsequently filled by emitting photons with different energies (colored curves). Once the pulse is gone, after a transient time of the order of tens of femtoseconds, the electronic states reach thermal equilibrium at a higher temperature than initially.
